So the last couple of days we’ve been talking a lot about solids.  We’ve been talking a lot about three dimensional shapes, and examples of what they look like, where we see them, where we can find them.  We also talked about how we can talk about them in terms of faces, edges, and vertices.  Today – and yesterday we explored a little bit about what these different solids look like from different angles, and sometimes, depending on the view we’re looking at, if we’re looking at the top, or looking at the side, or the front, we can’t see all the angles.  So today we’re going to take it a step further and begin talking about volume.  I’ll be having you put these into your notebooks later on, but for today I want you to just think about them.  I also have them posted on the side of the room as you’re working today just to keep it in mind.  Volume is the space and side inside a solid figure, and we measure it in cubic – I spelled cubic wrong – cubic units.  Cubic units are the volume of a cube whether there’s one unit on each side.  So today we’re going to be using these centimeter cubes, and you can see how tiny they are, and when we’re talking about cubic units, each side of these measure one unit, or in this case, the unit we’re talking about are centimeters.  Other units might be inches, meters, miles, yards, feet.  Today we’re talking about – these are cubic centimeters.  We’re just going to use the term cubic units for today.  Units is just a way of measuring things, and each one if we measure it would be one on each side.  Thinking about some of the other vocabulary we’ve been using this week, we’ve been talking about three dimensional shapes. We’ve been talking about solids, and also, what is the meaning of three dimensional?  If we have three dimensional shapes, what are we talking about it has to it?
